questions: An early-2000s beginner UNIX/Linux tutorial created by M. Stonebank at a UK university is distributed under a specific Creative Commons license. Identify the exact Creative Commons license version used for that tutorial and comprehensively describe its key components. Your answer should explain: (1) the freedoms granted to users, (2) the conditions imposed on reuse, (3) any stated limitations or disclaimers (including treatment of public domain elements and warranties), and (4) how this license version is characterized in relation to the newer 4.0 Creative Commons licenses.

A long-standing beginner-friendly tutorial on UNIX and Linux is distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike license, but readers are advised that a newer, more internationally robust version of that same license family is now strongly recommended for creators licensing their own work. Identify the current recommended version of the Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike license in the 4.0 suite and provide: (1) its canonical URL; (2) the core freedoms it grants to the public; (3) the main conditions imposed on reuse; and (4) several key improvements introduced in the 4.0 version compared to earlier 3.0 and unported licenses, including how it handles international enforceability, database rights, attribution flexibility, moral/publicity rights, and the cure period for license violations.

rounds.question: An early-2000s beginner UNIX/Linux tutorial created by M. Stonebank at a UK university is distributed under a specific Creative Commons license. Identify the exact Creative Commons license version used for that tutorial and comprehensively describe its key components. Your answer should explain: (1) the freedoms granted to users, (2) the conditions imposed on reuse, (3) any stated limitations or disclaimers (including treatment of public domain elements and warranties), and (4) how this license version is characterized in relation to the newer 4.0 Creative Commons licenses.
checklist_width: Correct identification of Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 license
Description of the freedoms: Share (copy and redistribute) and Adapt (remix, transform, build upon)
Statement that freedoms are irrevocable provided terms are followed
Attribution requirement (credit, link to license, indicate changes, no implied endorsement)
NonCommercial restriction (no commercial use)
ShareAlike requirement (derivatives must use same license)
No additional restrictions clause
Note about exceptions for public domain or applicable legal limitations
Disclaimer of warranties and mention of other rights (e.g., privacy, publicity, moral rights)
Explanation that this is an older license version and recommendation to use 4.0 versions instead, described as more user-friendly and internationally robust
checklist_depth: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 + matches the license applied to the early-2000s M. Stonebank UNIX/Linux tutorial
Recognition that the license is explicitly identified as an older version compared to the 4.0 CC licenses
rounds.rationale: The question uses contextual clues (early-2000s UNIX/Linux tutorial by M. Stonebank at a UK university) to require identification of the specific Creative Commons license applied (Deep reasoning). It then requires aggregation of multiple dispersed elements of the license deed—freedoms, conditions, disclaimers, and version comparison—ensuring broad information synthesis across the full license description (Wide coverage).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Notice This is an older version of this license. Compared to previous versions, the 4.0 versions of all CC licenses are more user-friendly and more internationally robust . If
rounds.answer_a:
M. Stonebank’s early-2000s UNIX/Linux tutorial (“UNIX Tutorial for Beginners”) is distributed under the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales** license, abbreviated **CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK: England & Wales**.[1][2]

This license grants broad freedoms. Users may **copy, distribute, display, and perform** the work, and may also **make derivative works** from it.[2] In practical terms, that means the tutorial can be shared, adapted, and remixed for teaching or learning, so long as the license terms are respected.

The reuse conditions are threefold.[2] First, **Attribution**: users must give appropriate credit in the manner specified by the author or licensor. Second, **NonCommercial**: the work may not be used for commercial purposes. Third, **ShareAlike**: if the tutorial is altered, transformed, or built upon, the resulting work must be distributed only under the same or a similar license. The license deed also notes that, for any reuse or distribution, the license terms must be made clear to others, although any condition can be waived with the copyright holder’s permission.[2]

The stated limitations/disclaimers are important. The deed says that **any rights arising from fair use, first sale, or other legal limitations are unaffected**.[2] It also states that **elements in the public domain or otherwise exempt from copyright need not comply with the license**.[2] Finally, Creative Commons emphasizes that the work is offered **without warranties**.[2]

Creative Commons now labels this version as an **“older” license**, recommends the **4.0 licenses for new works**, and explains that few ports were made after 3.0 because 4.0 is intended to operate internationally.[3]

## References
1. University of Surrey, *UNIX Tutorial for Beginners* (M. Stonebank), license statement. https://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
2. Creative Commons, *Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales*. https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/
3. Creative Commons, *About The Licenses* / legacy-license guidance indicating 4.0 is recommended for new works. https://creativecommons.org/licenses/
rounds.answer_b:
The tutorial is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK) licence [1].

1. Freedoms granted  
• Share – users may copy, distribute and transmit the tutorial in any medium or format.  
• Adapt – users may remix, transform or build upon it.  
These freedoms are irrevocable provided the licence terms are met and no technological restrictions (DRM) are added [2].

2. Conditions on reuse  
• Attribution – credit “M. Stonebank, University of Surrey” (or equivalent) and provide a link to the licence.  
• NonCommercial – no material may be used “for commercial advantage or private monetary compensation.”  
• ShareAlike – adaptations must be distributed under the same CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK licence (or a later compatible version).  
• No additional restrictions – licensees may not impose legal or technical barriers that limit the freedoms granted to others [2].

3. Limitations and disclaimers  
• Public-domain elements (e.g. facts, formulas) are not covered; users may exploit them without the licence.  
• No warranties are given; the work is provided “as-is” and the licensor disclaims all liability.  
• The licence does not affect statutory exceptions such as fair dealing, and moral rights are not waived.  
• Violation of any condition automatically terminates the licence, though it can be reinstated upon remedying the breach [2].

4. Relation to CC 4.0 licences  
CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK is a jurisdiction-specific, “legacy” licence. Version 4.0 replaced local ports with a single global text, adds explicit coverage of database and moral rights, simplifies attribution, and strengthens waiver and termination provisions. While works under 2.0 remain valid, Creative Commons recommends 4.0 for new publications because it is clearer, internationally harmonised and more enforceable across legal systems [3].

## References  
[1] “Unix & Linux Tutorial for Beginners,” footer, University of Surrey, accessed Apr 2026.  
[2] Creative Commons, “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales – Licence Deed,” creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/, accessed Apr 2026.  
[3] Creative Commons, “Why Upgrade to 4.0,” Creative Commons Blog, 2013.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify the license as Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales and satisfy the DEEP logic requirement. Both cover the major WIDE checklist elements: freedoms (share/adapt), attribution, noncommercial, share-alike, public domain exceptions, warranties, and comparison to 4.0.

However, Agent B is more comprehensive and precise. It explicitly states that freedoms are irrevocable provided terms are met, clearly articulates the “no additional restrictions” clause (including DRM), mentions termination and reinstatement, and references moral rights and database-right improvements in 4.0. Its explanation of how 4.0 differs (global, harmonized, clearer attribution, stronger provisions) is more detailed and user-informative.

In terms of presentation, Agent B uses clearer section numbering and bullet formatting, making it more scannable and aligned with modern search UX expectations. Agent A is accurate but slightly less detailed (e.g., no explicit mention of irrevocability or DRM restrictions) and somewhat less structured.

Therefore, Agent B provides the better overall user experience without any significant factual errors.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question: A long-standing beginner-friendly tutorial on UNIX and Linux is distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike license, but readers are advised that a newer, more internationally robust version of that same license family is now strongly recommended for creators licensing their own work. Identify the current recommended version of the Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike license in the 4.0 suite and provide: (1) its canonical URL; (2) the core freedoms it grants to the public; (3) the main conditions imposed on reuse; and (4) several key improvements introduced in the 4.0 version compared to earlier 3.0 and unported licenses, including how it handles international enforceability, database rights, attribution flexibility, moral/publicity rights, and the cure period for license violations.
checklist_width: Canonical URL for Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/)
Freedom to Share — copy and redistribute in any medium or format
Freedom to Adapt — remix, transform, and build upon the material
Condition: Attribution (credit, link to license, indicate changes)
Condition: NonCommercial (no commercial use)
Condition: ShareAlike (distribute adaptations under same license)
Condition: No additional restrictions
4.0 improvement: globally enforceable and no porting required
4.0 improvement: explicit coverage of sui generis database rights
4.0 improvement: waiver/clarification of moral, publicity, and personality rights where possible
4.0 improvement: attribution may be satisfied via link to a separate page
4.0 improvement: 30-day automatic reinstatement (cure period) for license violations
4.0 improvement: increased readability and clearer structure
checklist_depth: Correct identification of Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International as the recommended newer version over earlier (e.g., 2.0 or 3.0) licenses
Recognition that the license belongs to the Creative Commons 4.0 license suite designed to be more user-friendly and internationally robust
rounds.rationale: The question uses the context of a UNIX/Linux beginner tutorial licensed under an older CC BY-NC-SA version to indirectly point to the newer 4.0 license (Deep reasoning). It avoids naming the specific document and instead requires identifying the correct license family and version. The query then demands aggregation of multiple categories of information—URL, freedoms, conditions, and numerous cross-cutting improvements introduced in 4.0—requiring consultation of both the license deed and explanatory materials (Wide scope).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Canonical URL https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/ See the legal code You are free to: Share — copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format Adapt —
rounds.answer_a:
The currently recommended license in this family is **Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike 4.0 International**, usually abbreviated **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0**. Its canonical URL is: **https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/**.[1]

It grants the public the freedom to **share** the material — copy and redistribute it in any medium or format — and to **adapt** it by remixing, transforming, and building upon it.[1] These permissions are intended to be irrevocable so long as users comply with the license terms.[1]

The main reuse conditions are straightforward. **Attribution** requires users to give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate whether changes were made.[1] **NonCommercial** limits use to non-commercial purposes.[1] **ShareAlike** requires adaptations to be distributed under the same license.[1] The license also prohibits applying additional legal or technological restrictions, such as DRM, that would prevent others from exercising the licensed freedoms.[1]

Compared with earlier 3.0 and unported licenses, version 4.0 was designed to be more globally robust. Creative Commons describes 4.0 as better suited for **international use and enforceability**, reducing the need for jurisdiction-specific “ported” versions.[2][3] It also expressly covers **sui generis database rights**, clarifying reuse rights for databases across jurisdictions where those rights exist.[2] Attribution became more practical and flexible: 4.0 allows licensors to request removal of attribution information in certain circumstances and streamlines how attribution may be given, including reasonable manners suited to the medium.[2] The license also addresses **moral rights, publicity rights, privacy rights, and similar personality rights** more clearly, explaining that such rights are not broadly licensed, though licensors waive or agree not to assert some rights to the limited extent necessary for license exercise.[1][2] Finally, 4.0 introduced an important **30-day cure period**: if a violation is corrected within 30 days of discovery, license rights are automatically reinstated.[1][2]

## References
1. Creative Commons, “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)”. https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
2. Creative Commons, “4.0 License Versions / wiki overview of changes and improvements”. https://wiki.creativecommons.org/wiki/4.0
3. Creative Commons, “About The Licenses”. https://creativecommons.org/licenses/
rounds.answer_b:
Creative Commons now recommends using the Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike 4.0 International licence (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0).  

1. Canonical licence URL  
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/ [1]

2. Core freedoms granted  
• Share – anyone may copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format.  
• Adapt – anyone may remix, transform and build upon the material for any non-commercial purpose.  
These permissions are irrevocable provided the licence terms are followed and no technological protection measures that restrict recipients’ exercise of the licence are applied [1].

3. Main conditions on reuse  
• Attribution – give appropriate credit, provide a link to the licence, and indicate if changes were made.  
• NonCommercial – use is limited to purposes not primarily intended for or directed toward commercial advantage or monetary compensation.  
• ShareAlike – adaptations must be licensed under the same CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 licence.  
• No additional restrictions – no legal or technical barriers may be added that limit the freedoms the licence grants to others [1].

4. Key improvements introduced in 4.0 over 3.0/unported licences  
• One-text global licence: 4.0 dispenses with jurisdiction-specific “ports,” improving international enforceability and reducing choice-of-law uncertainty [2].  
• Explicit coverage of sui generis database rights: users receive the same share/adapt freedoms for protected databases, eliminating ambiguity present in earlier versions [2][3].  
• More flexible attribution: credit may be provided in any “reasonable manner,” and links may substitute for full bibliographic data, easing compliance for digital formats and small-screen devices [1][2].  
• Moral and publicity rights: licensors agree not to assert (or to waive to the extent possible) moral rights and publicity/privacy rights that would interfere with the licence, giving users greater legal certainty across jurisdictions [2].  
• Cure period for violations: if a user breaches the licence, rights terminate automatically but are reinstated if the breach is remedied within 30 days of discovery, reducing the risk of permanent loss for inadvertent mistakes [1][3].  

These changes make CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 more internationally robust, technologically neutral and user-friendly than its predecessors.

## References  
[1] Creative Commons. “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International—Licence Deed.” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/ (accessed Apr. 2026).  
[2] Creative Commons Wiki. “4.0 Licence Suite – Significant Improvements.” https://wiki.creativecommons.org/wiki/4.0 (accessed Apr. 2026).  
[3] Creative Commons Blog. “CC 4.0: A More Global, Interoperable Licence Suite,” 25 Nov 2013. https://creativecommons.org/2013/11/25/cc4-is-here.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0) as the recommended version in the 4.0 suite. Both clearly situate it within the Creative Commons 4.0 license family and explain its international robustness. ✅✅

Second, Width/Completeness: Both provide the canonical URL, core freedoms (Share, Adapt), all main conditions (Attribution, NonCommercial, ShareAlike, No additional restrictions), and key 4.0 improvements: global enforceability without porting, explicit coverage of sui generis database rights, clarification/waiver of moral and publicity rights, attribution flexibility (including link-based attribution), and the 30-day automatic reinstatement cure period. Both are accurate and comprehensive. Neither explicitly highlights “increased readability and clearer structure” as a standalone improvement, but both imply improved usability. No major checklist failures.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Agent B provides clearer section numbering (1–4), tighter bullet formatting, and more scannable structure aligned exactly with the four requested deliverables. The layout makes it easier to extract each required element in seconds. Agent A is accurate and well-written but more paragraph-heavy and slightly less structured.

Conclusion: Both are high-quality and factually strong, but Agent B offers superior formatting and scannability. Hence, [[B_BETTER]] with no substantive failure from Agent A.
